reasoning: The enforcement of this rule happens at the state machine level, not inside tls_process_key_update itself. In ossl_statem_client13_read_transition (statem_clnt.c lines 180-196), the transition to TLS_ST_CR_KEY_UPDATE is only permitted when the current hand_state is TLS_ST_OK: `case TLS_ST_OK: ... if (mt == SSL3_MT_KEY_UPDATE && !SSL_IS_QUIC_HANDSHAKE(s)) { st->hand_state = TLS_ST_CR_KEY_UPDATE; return 1; }`. The state TLS_ST_OK is only entered after the handshake is complete (including processing Finished). Similarly, in ossl_statem_server13_read_transition (statem_srvr.c lines 148-171), the transition to TLS_ST_SR_KEY_UPDATE is only permitted from TLS_ST_OK. If a KeyUpdate message arrives in any other state (i.e., before Finished has been received and TLS_ST_OK has been reached), the read transition function returns 0, which causes the state machine to generate an unexpected_message alert and terminate the connection. This is the standard OpenSSL state machine behavior when no valid transition is found — returning 0 from a read_transition function triggers a fatal alert. Therefore, KeyUpdate received before Finished will be rejected with unexpected_message, conforming to RFC 8446 section 4.6.3.

evidence.expected_behavior: KeyUpdate received before Finished must cause the connection to be terminated with an unexpected_message alert.
